1: <header>Environmental balance of the UK biogas sector: An evaluation by consequential life cycle assessment </header>
David Styles * , Eduardo Mesa Dominguez, Dave Chadwick School of Environment, Natural Resources and Geography, Bangor, Gwynedd LL57 2UW, UK

1: <header>A R T I C E INFO </header>
Article history: Received 30 January 2016 Received in revised form 30 March 2016 Accepted 30 March 2016 Available online 19 April 2016
Keywords: Anaerobic digestion GHG LCA Waste management Bioenergy Resource efficiency
2: Anaerobic digestion (AD) is expanding rapidly in the UK. Previous life cycle assessment (LCA) studies have highlighted the sensitivity of environmental outcomes to feedstock type, fugitive emissions, biomethane use, energy conversion efficiency and digestate management. We combined statistics on current and planned AD deployment with operational data from a survey of biogas plant operators to evaluate the environmental balance of the UK biogas sector for the years 2014 and 2017. Consequential LCA was applied to account for all major environmental credits and burdens incurred, including: (i) substitution of composting, incineration, sewer disposal, field decomposition and animal feeding of wastes; (ii) indirect land use change (ILUC) incurred by the cultivation of crops used for biogas production and to compensate for bakery and brewery wastes diverted from animal feed. In 2014, the UK biogas sector reduced greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by 551-755 Gg CO2e excluding ILUC, or 238-755 Gg CO2e including ILUC uncertainty. Fossil energy depletion was reduced by 8.9-10.8 PJe, but eutrophication and acidification burdens were increased by 1.8-3.4 Gg PO4e and 8.1-14.6 Gg SO2e, respectively. Food waste and manure feedstocks dominate GHG abatement, largely through substitution of in-vessel composting and manure storage, whilst food waste and crop feedstocks dominate fossil energy credit, primarily through substitution of natural gas power generation. Biogas expansion is projected to increase environmental credits and loadings by a factor of 2.4 by 2017. If all AD bioelectricity replaced coal generation, or if 90% of biomethane replaced transport diesel or grid natural gas, GHG abatement would increase by 131%, 38% and 20%, respectively. Policies to encourage digestion of food waste and manures could maximize GHG abatement, avoiding the risk of carbon leakage associated with use of crops and wastes otherwise used to feed livestock. Covering digestate stores could largely mitigate net eutrophication and acidification burdens.

4: Anaerobic digestion (AD) is an established technology to treat wet organic wastes that is increasingly being deployed as a renewable ener- gy technology across Europe to convert a range of feedstocks into biogas and ultimately bioelectricity, bioheat or transport fuel. This has arisen largely in response to energy-related subsidies paid to AD operators, such as feed-in-tariffs (FITs) paid for bioelectricity and the Renewable Heat Incentive (RHI) paid for bioheat (OFGEM, 2014). Up until 2014, almost all biogas produced in the UK was used to fuel combined heat and power (CHP) engines, producing bioelectricity eligible for a FIT subsidy of up to £0.10/kWh in 2015 (OFGEM, 2016). In 2014, there were 32 small-scale plants (<250 kWe) with a cumulative installed capacity of 3.4 MWe, 48 medium scale plants (>250 kWe 500 kWe) with a cumulative installed capacity of 22.6 MWe, and 72 large-scale plants 00 kWe) with a cumulative installed capacity of 136.0 MWe (NNFCC, 2014a, 2014b). The overall efficiency of electricity generation can vary significantly across AD-CHP plants (Bacenetti et al., 2013), influencing their performance in terms of greenhouse gas (GHG) abatement and fossil fuel substitution. Similarly, the magnitude of eu- trophication and acidification burdens arising largely from ammonia (NH3) emissions during the storage and field application of digestate are highly dependent on the type digestate storage infrastructure and method of digestate application (Boulamanti et al., 2013: Styles et al., 2015a, 2015b). However, up until now there has been little or no pub- lished information on the conversion efficiency of biogas feedstock into electricity, or on the methods of storing and spreading the residual digestate produced, for the UK biogas sector as a whole.

A review of 15 attributional life cycle assessment (LCA) studies (Hijazi et al., 2016) found that biogas energy has a lower GHG intensity than fossil reference energy, but can increase acidification and eutrophi- cation burdens. Whiting and Azapagic (2014) reported that biogas produced from agricultural wastes resulted in significant GHG abate- ment when it substituted natural gas in CHP engines, but increased acidification and eutrophication burdens by 25 and 12 fold, respectively. Whilst high biogas yields from maize have been found to support generation of bioelectricity with a considerably lower GHG intensity than grid-electricity in Germany and the UK (Whiting and Azapagic, 2014; Claus et al., 2014), maize biogas was also found to increase environmental burdens across eight out of 11 impact categories compared with natural gas (Whiting and Azapagic, 2014). However, fugitive emissions of methane (CH4) are highly variable (Adams et al., 2015), and may significantly reduce net GHG abatement achieved by crop-biogas. Furthermore, the aforementioned attributional LCA studies did not expand system boundaries to account for important indirect effects of AD system deployment, including the diversion of organic waste from alternative fates such as landfill or composting, and indirect land use change (ILUC) associated with the displacement of food crop production. Such effects could have an important influence on the environmental outcome of AD deployment at the national level, and may be captured by consequential LCA that accounts for indirect ef- fects caused by market signals arising from an intervention (Weidema, 2001).

Styles et al. (2015a) applied consequential LCA to highlight the risk of carbon leakage from ILUC when animal feed is diverted to bio- gas plants on dairy farms, building on the recognised need to expand LCA boundaries in order to accurately evaluate the environmental ef- ficiency of biogas and other renewable energy options (Rehl et al., 2012; Tufvesson et al., 2013; Tonini et al., 2016). Numerous recent studies have indicated that crop-biogas may actually increase GHG emissions when replacing fossil energy, if ILUC is incurred (Tonini et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2012; Styles et al., 2015b). LCA system boundaries have also been expanded to quantify significant GHG credits for biogas plants attributable to the avoidance of manure storage and waste management (e.g. composting), and fertilizer re- placement by digestate (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007; Bacenetti

et al., 2013; Boulamanti et al., 2013). For example, despite limited bioenergy yields (Lijó et al., 2014a), the digestion of pig slurry can achieve GHG abatement of 1.19 kg COe per kWh of electricity generated when avoided slurry storage emissions are accounted for as a credit (Bacenetti et al., 2013).

Bywater (2011) characterized small-scale farm AD-CHP plants <250 kWe capacity in the UK, typically co-digesting animal manures and crops, with single-stage digestion and relatively short residence times, often storing digestate in pre-existing manure storage facilities. Larger AD plants usually digest waste and crop feedstocks, and are more likely to be optimized for electricity generation with e.g. two- stage digestion and longer residence times. Given recent findings on large differences in life cycle environmental performance for different types of feedstock and operating parameters, quantifying the overall environmental balance of AD deployment in the UK could provide useful insight for policy makers on the efficacy of policies relating to AD, in terms of climate change, energy security, and air and water pollution. Consequential LCA is the most relevant approach for such an evaluation because it accounts for direct and indirect environmental burdens and credits associated with the introduction of AD, considering process substitution (Weidema, 2001; Tufvesson et al., 2013; Tonini et al., 2016).

A detailed statistical overview of the UK biogas sector has recently been provided by NNFCC (2014a, 2014b), including information on regional deployment, plant scale, feedstock requirements and land requirements, alongside projected biogas capacity development up until 2017. However, that overview did not include information on de- sign and operational parameters critical to environmental performance. We compiled a uniquely detailed profile of the non-wastewater- treatment-plant (non-WWTP) biogas sector in the UK by combining plant size and feedstock data (NNFCC, 2014a, 2014b) with operational data obtained from a survey of UK biogas plants. We then applied con- sequential LCA to evaluate the net environmental balance of the sector, and to explore alternative scenarios of deployment. This paper presents results from the biogas plant survey, and describes how they were used to estimate the environmental profile of the UK biogas sector through consequential LCA.

Styles et al. (2015a) applied consequential LCA to highlight the risk of carbon leakage from ILUC when animal feed is diverted to bio- gas plants on dairy farms, building on the recognised need to expand LCA boundaries in order to accurately evaluate the environmental ef- ficiency of biogas and other renewable energy options (Rehl et al., 2012; Tufvesson et al., 2013; Tonini et al., 2016). Numerous recent studies have indicated that crop-biogas may actually increase GHG emissions when replacing fossil energy, if ILUC is incurred (Tonini et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2012; Styles et al., 2015b). LCA system boundaries have also been expanded to quantify significant GHG credits for biogas plants attributable to the avoidance of manure storage and waste management (e.g. composting), and fertilizer re- placement by digestate (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007; Bacenetti

et al., 2013; Boulamanti et al., 2013). For example, despite limited bioenergy yields (Lijó et al., 2014a), the digestion of pig slurry can achieve GHG abatement of 1.19 kg COe per kWh of electricity generated when avoided slurry storage emissions are accounted for as a credit (Bacenetti et al., 2013).

Bywater (2011) characterized small-scale farm AD-CHP plants <250 kWe capacity in the UK, typically co-digesting animal manures and crops, with single-stage digestion and relatively short residence times, often storing digestate in pre-existing manure storage facilities. Larger AD plants usually digest waste and crop feedstocks, and are more likely to be optimized for electricity generation with e.g. two- stage digestion and longer residence times. Given recent findings on large differences in life cycle environmental performance for different types of feedstock and operating parameters, quantifying the overall environmental balance of AD deployment in the UK could provide useful insight for policy makers on the efficacy of policies relating to AD, in terms of climate change, energy security, and air and water pollution. Consequential LCA is the most relevant approach for such an evaluation because it accounts for direct and indirect environmental burdens and credits associated with the introduction of AD, considering process substitution (Weidema, 2001; Tufvesson et al., 2013; Tonini et al., 2016).

A detailed statistical overview of the UK biogas sector has recently been provided by NNFCC (2014a, 2014b), including information on regional deployment, plant scale, feedstock requirements and land requirements, alongside projected biogas capacity development up until 2017. However, that overview did not include information on de- sign and operational parameters critical to environmental performance. We compiled a uniquely detailed profile of the non-wastewater- treatment-plant (non-WWTP) biogas sector in the UK by combining plant size and feedstock data (NNFCC, 2014a, 2014b) with operational data obtained from a survey of UK biogas plants. We then applied con- sequential LCA to evaluate the net environmental balance of the sector, and to explore alternative scenarios of deployment. This paper presents results from the biogas plant survey, and describes how they were used to estimate the environmental profile of the UK biogas sector through consequential LCA.
9: The aim of this study was to quantify the net environmental balance (burden change) associated with recent and projected deployment of AD in the UK, considering incurred and avoided processes and emissions. The study involved the development and application of the LCAD EcoScreen tool, applying a consequential LCA approach to calculate the life cycle environmental balance of AD in relation to a reference flow of 1 Mg of dry matter (DM) feedstock input, for a range of charac- terized feedstocks, plant operating characteristics and counterfactual (substituted) material flows. Major processes accounted for are shown in Fig. 1. The construction and manufacture of buildings and equipment were excluded from the scope, as is typical for bioenergy carbon footprints 2009).

Results were expressed in relation to four environmental impact cat- egories based on CML (2010) characterization factors: global warming potential (GWP) expressed as COe, eutrophication potential (EP) expressed as PO4e, acidification potential (AP) expressed as SO2e, and fossil resource depletion potential (FRDP) expressed as MJe. For exam- ple, 100-yr GWP factors for CH4 and N20 are 25 and 298, respectively. Scenario results were normalized against reported annual UK loadings for GWP (DECC, 2015), and against estimated UK loadings for EP, AP and FRDP after per capita extrapolation of EU loadings (Sleeswijk et al., 2008) based on EU 28 and UK population numbers of 510 million and 65 million, respectively (Eurostat, 2016).

12: LCA was undertaken for 1 Mg DM across 16 types of feedstock, and a total of 77 permutations of feedstock and plant operating parameters, based on national quantities of biogas feedstocks and plant operating characteristics described in the subsequent sections. Table 1 summa- rizes the framework methodology employed, modified from Styles et al. (2015a, 2015b). Inputs accounted for in detailed attributional LCA studies found to be of minor relevance to the four impact categories studied, such as water, lubricating oil and sodium hydroxide (e.g. Poeschl et al., 2012), were disregarded; the aim was to accurately capture wider environmental effects of the sector as a whole.

Burden data for key inputs and processes are summarized in Table 2. Crop cultivation burdens were based on Styles et al. (2015b) for UK cultivation of maize and grass, and from Ecoinvent v3.1 (Weidema et al., 2013) for fodder beet, and for rye as a proxy for "other cereal silage" (Table 2). The effect of possible land transformation caused by displacement of food production by cultivation of biogas crops, or by additional cultivation of wheat to compensate for animal feed diverted to AD, was evaluated for the purposes of uncertainty analyses. The ILUC factors proposed in Styles et al. (2015b) for land transformation at the global agricultural frontier were applied to an area equivalent to 100% of the area occupied by the biogas or compensatory wheat-feed crops. These factors equate to a GWP burden of 25 Mg yr CO2e and an EP burden of 9.9 kg PO4e respectively, per hectare transformed, based on IPCC (2006) default LUC factors (representing a worst case effect). Methodology and embodied burdens are further elaborated in the following sections, and in SI Section 3.

14: The LCAD EcoScreen tool was run for the annual quantities of feed- stock required for all UK non-WWTP biogas plants operational as of October 2014, reported by NNFCC (2014b). The apportionment of re- ported feedstock quantities across 16 types and five main categories of feedstock material (Table 3) is summarized in SI Section 2. In line with the CLCA objectives, only net additional transport incurred for each feedstock was used to calculate biogas sector transport burdens. Thus, no additional transport burdens were incurred for manure feedstocks compared with baseline manure management, nor for onsite processing of wet wastes. Crops and other waste feedstocks incurred transport
burdens of 5 and 50 km, respectively. In all cases except for manures it was assumed that digestates incurred additional transport burdens of 5 km for field application.
15: As of April 2014, 138 non-WWTP AD plants were operational in the UK (NNFCC, 2014a). An anonymous postal questionnaire was sent to the 78 AD plants for which postal addresses could be found online. The questionnaire contained 30 questions over five sections, covering: feedstock characteristics and quantities; heat and electricity generation and use; digestate storage and application details; operational issues such as maintenance requirements; operator perspectives on AD based on their experience. Twenty-four responses were received to the questionnaire survey relating to 26 biogas-CHP plants which were categorized according to size as per NNFCC (2014a): eight small plants (50-250 kWe); seven medium-sized plants (350-500 kWe); 11 large plants 1000 kWe). Not all respondents answered all questions, leaving a reduced number of data points for some parameters. Key results from the survey are summarized in Table SI 1.1 and Fig. 2.

Electricity conversion efficiency (Nelec) was estimated by dividing reported gross electricity generation (Gelec, MJ) by the lower heating value (LHV) of biomethane produced (LHV biomethane MJ), which was es- timated based on feedstock inputs (Qfeedstock, Mg) for feedstocks i-n, using estimated default biomethane yields (Ybiomethane m³/Mg) reported in Table 3, a density of 0.67 kg and a LHV of 50 MJ/kg for methane: Ybiomethane i-n) 0.67 X 50].
Values ranged from 0.27 to 0.57 (Fig. 2), representing variation in actual net biomethane yields and CHP transformation efficiencies.

Non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis analyses were undertaken in IBM SPSS Statistics 22 to test for differences in the distribution of values across AD plant size categories for the following parameters: estimated biomethane electricity conversion efficiency, parasitic electricity requirement, parasitic heat requirement, and the fraction of CHP heat output that replaces fossil-fuel heating. Statistically significant size ef- fects were confirmed for electricity conversion efficiency (p = 0.007) and the fraction of surplus CHP heat output replacing fossil fuel heating (p = 0.015) (Table SI 1.1; Fig. 2). Median values for each size category were applied in the national LCA evaluation for these parameters. Esti- mated biomethane-to-electricity conversion efficiencies (Fig. 2;

Based on median conver- sion efficiencies (Table SI 1.1) and assuming constant CHP electrical effi- ciencies of 35% and 40% for small and medium/large scale biogas plants, respectively (Bacenetti et al., 2013), biomethane yields in Table 3 were multiplied by 0.91, 1.10 and 1.19 in order to calculate biomethane yields for small, medium and large plants, respectively, in the national LCA. Fractions of CHP electricity and heat output required to run the AD plant (parasitic loads), were not statistically different across size catego- ries (Table SI 1.1), so overall median values were used for all sizes of biogas-CHP plant considered in the national LCA evaluation.

Table SI 1.1) indicate that biomethane yields vary from default values presented in Table 3 depending on plant size. Based on median conver- sion efficiencies (Table SI 1.1) and assuming constant CHP electrical effi- ciencies of 35% and 40% for small and medium/large scale biogas plants, respectively (Bacenetti et al., 2013), biomethane yields in Table 3 were multiplied by 0.91, 1.10 and 1.19 in order to calculate biomethane yields for small, medium and large plants, respectively, in the national LCA. Fractions of CHP electricity and heat output required to run the AD plant (parasitic loads), were not statistically different across size catego- ries (Table SI 1.1), so overall median values were used for all sizes of biogas-CHP plant considered in the national LCA evaluation.
17: As of April 2014, 138 non-WWTP AD plants were operational in the UK (NNFCC, 2014a). An anonymous postal questionnaire was sent to the 78 AD plants for which postal addresses could be found online. The questionnaire contained 30 questions over five sections, covering: feedstock characteristics and quantities; heat and electricity generation and use; digestate storage and application details; operational issues such as maintenance requirements; operator perspectives on AD based on their experience. Twenty-four responses were received to the questionnaire survey relating to 26 biogas-CHP plants which were categorized according to size as per NNFCC (2014a): eight small plants (50-250 kWe); seven medium-sized plants (350-500 kWe); 11 large plants 1000 kWe). Not all respondents answered all questions, leaving a reduced number of data points for some parameters. Key results from the survey are summarized in Table SI 1.1 and Fig. 2.

Electricity conversion efficiency (Nelec) was estimated by dividing reported gross electricity generation (Gelec, MJ) by the lower heating value (LHV) of biomethane produced (LHV biomethane MJ), which was es- timated based on feedstock inputs (Qfeedstock, Mg) for feedstocks i-n, using estimated default biomethane yields (Ybiomethane m³/Mg) reported in Table 3, a density of 0.67 kg and a LHV of 50 MJ/kg for methane: Ybiomethane i-n) 0.67 X 50].
Values ranged from 0.27 to 0.57 (Fig. 2), representing variation in actual net biomethane yields and CHP transformation efficiencies.

Non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis analyses were undertaken in IBM SPSS Statistics 22 to test for differences in the distribution of values across AD plant size categories for the following parameters: estimated biomethane electricity conversion efficiency, parasitic electricity requirement, parasitic heat requirement, and the fraction of CHP heat output that replaces fossil-fuel heating. Statistically significant size ef- fects were confirmed for electricity conversion efficiency (p = 0.007) and the fraction of surplus CHP heat output replacing fossil fuel heating (p = 0.015) (Table SI 1.1; Fig. 2). Median values for each size category were applied in the national LCA evaluation for these parameters. Esti- mated biomethane-to-electricity conversion efficiencies (Fig. 2;

Table SI 1.1) indicate that biomethane yields vary from default values presented in Table 3 depending on plant size. Based on median conver- sion efficiencies (Table SI 1.1) and assuming constant CHP electrical effi- ciencies of 35% and 40% for small and medium/large scale biogas plants, respectively (Bacenetti et al., 2013), biomethane yields in Table 3 were multiplied by 0.91, 1.10 and 1.19 in order to calculate biomethane yields for small, medium and large plants, respectively, in the national LCA. Fractions of CHP electricity and heat output required to run the AD plant (parasitic loads), were not statistically different across size catego- ries (Table SI 1.1), so overall median values were used for all sizes of biogas-CHP plant considered in the national LCA evaluation.
20: As of April 2014, 138 non-WWTP AD plants were operational in the UK (NNFCC, 2014a). An anonymous postal questionnaire was sent to the 78 AD plants for which postal addresses could be found online. The questionnaire contained 30 questions over five sections, covering: feedstock characteristics and quantities; heat and electricity generation and use; digestate storage and application details; operational issues such as maintenance requirements; operator perspectives on AD based on their experience. Twenty-four responses were received to the questionnaire survey relating to 26 biogas-CHP plants which were categorized according to size as per NNFCC (2014a): eight small plants (50-250 kWe); seven medium-sized plants (350-500 kWe); 11 large plants 1000 kWe). Not all respondents answered all questions, leaving a reduced number of data points for some parameters. Key results from the survey are summarized in Table SI 1.1 and Fig. 2.

Electricity conversion efficiency (Nelec) was estimated by dividing reported gross electricity generation (Gelec, MJ) by the lower heating value (LHV) of biomethane produced (LHV biomethane MJ), which was es- timated based on feedstock inputs (Qfeedstock, Mg) for feedstocks i-n, using estimated default biomethane yields (Ybiomethane m³/Mg) reported in Table 3, a density of 0.67 kg and a LHV of 50 MJ/kg for methane: Ybiomethane i-n) 0.67 X 50].
Values ranged from 0.27 to 0.57 (Fig. 2), representing variation in actual net biomethane yields and CHP transformation efficiencies.

Non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis analyses were undertaken in IBM SPSS Statistics 22 to test for differences in the distribution of values across AD plant size categories for the following parameters: estimated biomethane electricity conversion efficiency, parasitic electricity requirement, parasitic heat requirement, and the fraction of CHP heat output that replaces fossil-fuel heating. Statistically significant size ef- fects were confirmed for electricity conversion efficiency (p = 0.007) and the fraction of surplus CHP heat output replacing fossil fuel heating (p = 0.015) (Table SI 1.1; Fig. 2). Median values for each size category were applied in the national LCA evaluation for these parameters. Esti- mated biomethane-to-electricity conversion efficiencies (Fig. 2;

Based on median conver- sion efficiencies (Table SI 1.1) and assuming constant CHP electrical effi- ciencies of 35% and 40% for small and medium/large scale biogas plants, respectively (Bacenetti et al., 2013), biomethane yields in Table 3 were multiplied by 0.91, 1.10 and 1.19 in order to calculate biomethane yields for small, medium and large plants, respectively, in the national LCA. Fractions of CHP electricity and heat output required to run the AD plant (parasitic loads), were not statistically different across size catego- ries (Table SI 1.1), so overall median values were used for all sizes of biogas-CHP plant considered in the national LCA evaluation.
23: As of April 2014, 138 non-WWTP AD plants were operational in the UK (NNFCC, 2014a). An anonymous postal questionnaire was sent to the 78 AD plants for which postal addresses could be found online. The questionnaire contained 30 questions over five sections, covering: feedstock characteristics and quantities; heat and electricity generation and use; digestate storage and application details; operational issues such as maintenance requirements; operator perspectives on AD based on their experience. Twenty-four responses were received to the questionnaire survey relating to 26 biogas-CHP plants which were categorized according to size as per NNFCC (2014a): eight small plants (50-250 kWe); seven medium-sized plants (350-500 kWe); 11 large plants 1000 kWe). Not all respondents answered all questions, leaving a reduced number of data points for some parameters. Key results from the survey are summarized in Table SI 1.1 and Fig. 2.

Electricity conversion efficiency (Nelec) was estimated by dividing reported gross electricity generation (Gelec, MJ) by the lower heating value (LHV) of biomethane produced (LHV biomethane MJ), which was es- timated based on feedstock inputs (Qfeedstock, Mg) for feedstocks i-n, using estimated default biomethane yields (Ybiomethane m³/Mg) reported in Table 3, a density of 0.67 kg and a LHV of 50 MJ/kg for methane: Ybiomethane i-n) 0.67 X 50].
Values ranged from 0.27 to 0.57 (Fig. 2), representing variation in actual net biomethane yields and CHP transformation efficiencies.

Non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis analyses were undertaken in IBM SPSS Statistics 22 to test for differences in the distribution of values across AD plant size categories for the following parameters: estimated biomethane electricity conversion efficiency, parasitic electricity requirement, parasitic heat requirement, and the fraction of CHP heat output that replaces fossil-fuel heating. Statistically significant size ef- fects were confirmed for electricity conversion efficiency (p = 0.007) and the fraction of surplus CHP heat output replacing fossil fuel heating (p = 0.015) (Table SI 1.1; Fig. 2). and Misselbrook et al. (2012) (Table 1). Cattle slurry is the dominant manure feedstock (Table 3). Data from the Defra farm practice survey indicate that 15% of dairy farms store liquid cattle slurry in a crusted tank, 32% in an open tank, and 53% in a lagoon (Defra, 2014b). Counter- factual management of cattle slurry was apportioned across these system types using the above percentages (Table 4). According to Defra (2014b), most "Pigs & Poultry" farms store their manure in a tank, so it was assumed that all pig and poultry slurry going to AD was diverted from "pit storage" and "liquid storage", respectively, as defined in IPCC (2006). It was assumed that all solid poultry litter going to AD was diverted from storage in manure heaps. Emissions and NPK fertiliz- er replacement for counterfactual manure spreading were calculated using the MANNER NPK model (Nicholson et al., 2013), assuming broadcast application in February and April to a spring crop, and June and September to an autumn crop, on a sandy-clay-loam soil. Medium and large biogas plants digest a wide range of feedstock (Table SI 1.1). In-vessel composting was considered to be the marginal type of waste management avoided by AD treatment of separately collected wet organic waste, such as food waste (Table 4). This reflects the dominant fate of separately collected organic waste, and represents a conservative long-term assumption given that a significant fraction of organic waste still goes to landfill in the UK (Mistry et al., 2011). Bakery and brewery wastes are highly valued as animal feeds; conservatively, it was assumed that these waste types were efficiently utilized to substitute wheat-based animal feed on an energy content basis before being diverted to AD (Table 4). Diversion to AD is thus associated with a possible ILUC effect, because additional wheat must be grown to

compensate for the lost animal feed (Tonini et al., 2016), as described in SI 3.4. Waste starch and cheese processing wastes are often liquid, with low solids content, and therefore difficult to transport for alterna- tive uses such as animal feed. These wastes were assumed to be disposed of as liquid effluent in the absence of on-site AD, to be treated in an off-site WWTP (Table 4). Conservatively, it was assumed that off- site wastewater treatment involved AD of sewage sludge, leading to an identical environmental balance to on-site AD (from a modelling per- spective, this leads to no net change in environmental burden whether wastewater is treated in an on-site or off-site AD plant). Owing to health and hygiene regulations surrounding the handling of animal processing waste, incineration was considered the most likely counterfactual fate for this waste stream (Table 4), modelled based on Ecoinvent v3.1 data (Table 2; SI 3.6). Finally, it was assumed that agricultural land used to cultivate biogas-crops would otherwise have been used to pro- duce food or animal feed. Displaced food or feed production may thus incur ILUC. Waste streams going to medium- and large-scale biogas plants were modelled in quantities determined by the aforementioned statistics and assumptions (Table 4).

and Misselbrook et al. (2012) (Table 1). Cattle slurry is the dominant manure feedstock (Table 3). Data from the Defra farm practice survey indicate that 15% of dairy farms store liquid cattle slurry in a crusted tank, 32% in an open tank, and 53% in a lagoon (Defra, 2014b). Counter- factual management of cattle slurry was apportioned across these system types using the above percentages (Table 4). According to Defra (2014b), most "Pigs & Poultry" farms store their manure in a tank, so it was assumed that all pig and poultry slurry going to AD was diverted from "pit storage" and "liquid storage", respectively, as defined in IPCC (2006). It was assumed that all solid poultry litter going to AD was diverted from storage in manure heaps. Emissions and NPK fertiliz- er replacement for counterfactual manure spreading were calculated using the MANNER NPK model (Nicholson et al., 2013), assuming broadcast application in February and April to a spring crop, and June and September to an autumn crop, on a sandy-clay-loam soil. Medium and large biogas plants digest a wide range of feedstock (Table SI 1.1). In-vessel composting was considered to be the marginal type of waste management avoided by AD treatment of separately collected wet organic waste, such as food waste (Table 4). This reflects the dominant fate of separately collected organic waste, and represents a conservative long-term assumption given that a significant fraction of organic waste still goes to landfill in the UK (Mistry et al., 2011). Bakery and brewery wastes are highly valued as animal feeds; conservatively, it was assumed that these waste types were efficiently utilized to substitute wheat-based animal feed on an energy content basis before being diverted to AD (Table 4). Diversion to AD is thus associated with a possible ILUC effect, because additional wheat must be grown to

compensate for the lost animal feed (Tonini et al., 2016), as described in SI 3.4. Waste starch and cheese processing wastes are often liquid, with low solids content, and therefore difficult to transport for alterna- tive uses such as animal feed. These wastes were assumed to be disposed of as liquid effluent in the absence of on-site AD, to be treated in an off-site WWTP (Table 4). Conservatively, it was assumed that off- site wastewater treatment involved AD of sewage sludge, leading to an identical environmental balance to on-site AD (from a modelling per- spective, this leads to no net change in environmental burden whether wastewater is treated in an on-site or off-site AD plant). Owing to health and hygiene regulations surrounding the handling of animal processing waste, incineration was considered the most likely counterfactual fate for this waste stream (Table 4), modelled based on Ecoinvent v3.1 data (Table 2; SI 3.6). Finally, it was assumed that agricultural land used to cultivate biogas-crops would otherwise have been used to pro- duce food or animal feed. Displaced food or feed production may thus incur ILUC. Waste streams going to medium- and large-scale biogas plants were modelled in quantities determined by the aforementioned statistics and assumptions (Table 4).

31: Digestate storage infrastructures for different biogas plant size cate- gories were apportioned as per the distribution across surveyed plants (Table SI 1.2). As shown in Table 1, digestate storage infrastructure strongly influences methane and ammonia emissions, although more complete digestion in medium and large-scale plants (indicated by

higher electricity yields) constrains methane emissions from digestate storage to 1.5% of biomethane yield irrespective of storage type in these plants (Lijó et al., 2014b). For medium and large biogas plants, in order to avoid bias associated with apportioning particular feedstocks to particular digestate storage assumptions, each feedstock type was apportioned across the digestate storage practices in proportion to their reported prevalence for each size category (Table 4). Uncertainty around fugitive emissions from digestate storage is high (Adams et al., 2015), represented by an uncertainty range of + 25% in uncertainty analyses.

To constrain the number of model runs whilst avoiding bias for partic- ular feedstocks, trailing hose application of digestate was assumed in all cases as a representative, average efficiency technique (Table SI 1.1) - approximating to trailing shoe and dribble bar, and between splash plate and injection methods, in terms of NH3 emissions and fertilizer-N- replacement that dominate the environmental profile of digestate application (Table 1). Emissions and NPK fertilizer replacement values were based on results for February and April application to a spring crop, and June and September application to an autumn crop, on sandy- clay-loam soils, modelled using MANNER NPK (Nicholson et al., 2013) parameterised with relevant digestate nutrient concentrations from Table 3.
32: Digestate storage infrastructures for different biogas plant size cate- gories were apportioned as per the distribution across surveyed plants (Table SI 1.2). As shown in Table 1, digestate storage infrastructure strongly influences methane and ammonia emissions, although more complete digestion in medium and large-scale plants (indicated by

higher electricity yields) constrains methane emissions from digestate storage to 1.5% of biomethane yield irrespective of storage type in these plants (Lijó et al., 2014b). For medium and large biogas plants, in order to avoid bias associated with apportioning particular feedstocks to particular digestate storage assumptions, each feedstock type was apportioned across the digestate storage practices in proportion to their reported prevalence for each size category (Table 4). Uncertainty around fugitive emissions from digestate storage is high (Adams et al., 2015), represented by an uncertainty range of + 25% in uncertainty analyses.

To constrain the number of model runs whilst avoiding bias for partic- ular feedstocks, trailing hose application of digestate was assumed in all cases as a representative, average efficiency technique (Table SI 1.1) - approximating to trailing shoe and dribble bar, and between splash plate and injection methods, in terms of NH3 emissions and fertilizer-N- replacement that dominate the environmental profile of digestate application (Table 1). Emissions and NPK fertilizer replacement values were based on results for February and April application to a spring crop, and June and September application to an autumn crop, on sandy- clay-loam soils, modelled using MANNER NPK (Nicholson et al., 2013) parameterised with relevant digestate nutrient concentrations from Table 3.
33: Despite the use of detailed survey information to parameterise the LCA modelling, significant uncertainty remains over some biogas-plant factors, such as fugitive emissions, and especially over expanded bound- ary processes. Digestate storage emissions and fossil energy replace- ment burdens were varied by + 25% and + 10%, respectively. High uncertainty over avoided manure management and waste management burdens for individual feedstocks may average out to lower aggregate uncertainty across all feedstock input at the national scale; nonetheless, national aggregate values were varied + 25%. Uncertainty ranges were calculated excluding and including highly uncertain ILUC burdens for all crops and feedstocks diverted from animal feed.

Four alternative deployment scenarios were run to test for sensi- tivity to alternative marginal technologies, and the magnitude of en- vironmental benefit associated with mitigation options. Relevant technologies and associated environmental burdens are summarized in Table 2.

Scenario I: The default marginal energy types replaced in the study are natural gas electricity generation via combined cycle turbine and oil heating (DECC, 2012). However, political drivers could lead to coal electricity generation becoming the marginal displaced technology (Finnveden et al., 2005). The possible effect of this was modelled using burdens for coal electricity generation in the UK taken from Ecoinvent v3.1 (Weidema et al., 2013).

Scenario II: Injection of biomethane into the natural gas grid was evaluated as an alternative to CHP in a simple scenario in which 90% of biogas produced nationally in medium and large scale biogas plants was upgraded and injected into the grid (10% combusted in onsite CHP to provide heat and power for the biogas plant). Environmental credits were calculated as avoided natural gas heating burdens from Ecoinvent v3.1 whilst incurred post-digestion burdens were calculated assuming 1.4% CH4 slip during biomethane upgrading (Ravina and Genon, 2015) and NO emissions from biomethane combustion in boilers taken from Ecoinvent v3.1.

Scenario III: The use of upgraded biomethane as a transport fuel was also considered, based on vehicle-km direct burdens only for large Euro V diesel cars powered by biomethane, taken from Ecoinvent v3.1, and avoiding double-counting of upstream burdens for biomethane produc- tion calculated in the LCAD EcoScreen tool. Environmental credits were calculated as vehicle-km burdens for large Euro V cars powered by diesel, taken from Ecoinvent v3.1, and assuming 1 MJ of biomethane replaces 0.75 M diesel on a LHV basis (VVT, 2012).
Scenario IV: The environmental improvement associated with the retro-fitting of floating covers to all open digestate stores was explored, assuming an 80% reduction in NH3 emissions (FNR, 2010).
39: Despite the use of detailed survey information to parameterise the LCA modelling, significant uncertainty remains over some biogas-plant factors, such as fugitive emissions, and especially over expanded bound- ary processes. Digestate storage emissions and fossil energy replace- ment burdens were varied by + 25% and + 10%, respectively. High uncertainty over avoided manure management and waste management burdens for individual feedstocks may average out to lower aggregate uncertainty across all feedstock input at the national scale; nonetheless, national aggregate values were varied + 25%. Uncertainty ranges were calculated excluding and including highly uncertain ILUC burdens for all crops and feedstocks diverted from animal feed.

41: Results for the environmental balance of the UK biogas sector in 2014 were conservatively extrapolated to projected deployment in 2017, assuming that all existing plants and 40% of planned AD plants (NNFCC, 2014b) would be operational in that year. The environmental balance for each feedstock in 2014 was scaled up according to the ratio of tonnage for each feedstock in 2017 versus 2014 (Table SI 2.1). This assumes a similar pattern of deployment across plant sizes in 2017 to 2014, and continued dominance of CHP for energy conversion.
42: 3.1. National balance of UK biogas sector in 2014

In 2014, deployment of AD in the UK reduced GHG emissions by 653 Gg CO2e, or 251 Gg CO2e if worst case ILUC from crop feedstock and diversion of residues from animal feed is accounted for, with a prob- able range of 238 to 755 Gg CO2e after propagating major uncertainties (Table 5). The main GWP credits, in order of decreasing magnitude, were substitution of marginal grid electricity, avoided waste manage- ment, substitution of fertilizers and avoided manure management (Fig. 3; Table SI 4.1). Crops represented 27% of feedstock DM and contributed 2% towards GWP savings excluding ILUC, or cancelled out 42% of GWP savings if worst case ILUC was accounted for. Manures and food waste represented 6% and 48% of feedstock DM, and contribut- ed 16% and 76% towards GWP savings from AD, respectively (Fig. 4 4). The digestion of brewery waste and bakery waste increased GWP burdens by 11.5 and 5.5 Gg yr CO2e, respectively, assuming these wastes were diverted from optimum counterfactual use as animal feed, thus in- curring compensatory cultivation of wheat feed. If this compensatory wheat cultivation in turn incurs ILUC, then net GWP burdens for these two waste fractions increase to 90.2 and 40.6 Gg yr respectively.

The conservative assumption that liquid cheese processing waste and waste starch were diverted from treatment in centralized WWTP AD plants meant that these feedstocks had no net effect on the environ- mental balance of the non-WWTP AD sector. Aside from crop cultivation and possible ILUC, main GWP burdens arose from digestate storage, digestate application, and fugitive CH4 leakage during fermentation (Fig. 3; Table 4.1).

Anaerobic digestion increased national annual eutrophication and acidification burdens by 2.6 Gg PO4e and 11.4 Gg SO2e in 2014 excluding ILUC, with ranges of 1.8 to 3.4 Gg PO4e and 8.1 to 14.6 Gg SO2e after propagating major uncertainties (Table 5). Crops, food waste and "other waste" contributed approximately one third each towards eutro- phication burdens, whilst food waste accounted for 55% of acidification burdens owing to its volume and high NH3 emissions from NH4-rich digestate (Fig. 4). Notably, digestate storage was found to be a particular hotspot in our analyses, owing to the use of NH4-driven NH3 emission factors for different storage systems (Table 1; Misselbrook et al., 2012) which appear to be significantly higher than digestate storage emission factors reported by Amon et al. (2006) and applied in other studies (e.g. Lijó et al., 2014a, 2014b). The higher NH4-N content and pH of digestates compared with slurries would suggest that NH3 emissions from digestate storage are likely to be higher than for slurry storage.

Despite their relatively small quantities, brewery and bakery waste made significant net contributions to the eutrophication burden of the AD sector, again owing to diversion from animal feed, incurring additional wheat cultivation. Dominant contributing processes were, in order of decreasing importance, digestate storage, digestate application, cultivation (for EP only), and CHP combustion (Fig. 3; Table SI 4.2 and Table 4.3). Avoided manure management and avoided waste manage- ment represented the main acidification and eutrophication credits.

In 2014, AD reduced fossil resource depletion in the UK by 9.8 PJe, with a range of 8.9 to 10.8 PJ (Table 5). Food waste and crops contribut- ed 62% and 22%, respectively, towards fossil resource savings. Burdens arising from waste transport, crop cultivation and avoided manure application (avoided fertilizer replacement) were small compared with the credits arising from substitution of marginal grid electricity (Fig. 3; Table SI 4.4). Digestate application (fertilizer replacement) and the avoidance of waste composting generated small but significant FRDP credits (Fig. 3).

The conservative assumption that liquid cheese processing waste and waste starch were diverted from treatment in centralized WWTP AD plants meant that these feedstocks had no net effect on the environ- mental balance of the non-WWTP AD sector. Aside from crop cultivation and possible ILUC, main GWP burdens arose from digestate storage, digestate application, and fugitive CH4 leakage during fermentation (Fig. 3; Table 4.1).

Anaerobic digestion increased national annual eutrophication and acidification burdens by 2.6 Gg PO4e and 11.4 Gg SO2e in 2014 excluding ILUC, with ranges of 1.8 to 3.4 Gg PO4e and 8.1 to 14.6 Gg SO2e after propagating major uncertainties (Table 5). Crops, food waste and "other waste" contributed approximately one third each towards eutro- phication burdens, whilst food waste accounted for 55% of acidification burdens owing to its volume and high NH3 emissions from NH4-rich digestate (Fig. 4). Notably, digestate storage was found to be a particular hotspot in our analyses, owing to the use of NH4-driven NH3 emission factors for different storage systems (Table 1; Misselbrook et al., 2012) which appear to be significantly higher than digestate storage emission factors reported by Amon et al. (2006) and applied in other studies (e.g. Lijó et al., 2014a, 2014b). The higher NH4-N content and pH of digestates compared with slurries would suggest that NH3 emissions from digestate storage are likely to be higher than for slurry storage.

Despite their relatively small quantities, brewery and bakery waste made significant net contributions to the eutrophication burden of the AD sector, again owing to diversion from animal feed, incurring additional wheat cultivation. Dominant contributing processes were, in order of decreasing importance, digestate storage, digestate application, cultivation (for EP only), and CHP combustion (Fig. 3; Table SI 4.2 and Table 4.3). Avoided manure management and avoided waste manage- ment represented the main acidification and eutrophication credits.

In 2014, AD reduced fossil resource depletion in the UK by 9.8 PJe, with a range of 8.9 to 10.8 PJ (Table 5). Food waste and crops contribut- ed 62% and 22%, respectively, towards fossil resource savings. Burdens arising from waste transport, crop cultivation and avoided manure application (avoided fertilizer replacement) were small compared with the credits arising from substitution of marginal grid electricity (Fig. 3; Table SI 4.4). Digestate application (fertilizer replacement) and the avoidance of waste composting generated small but significant FRDP credits (Fig. 3).

Digestate application (fertilizer replacement) and the avoidance of waste composting generated small but significant FRDP credits (Fig. 3).

The conservative assumption that liquid cheese processing waste and waste starch were diverted from treatment in centralized WWTP AD plants meant that these feedstocks had no net effect on the environ- mental balance of the non-WWTP AD sector. Aside from crop cultivation and possible ILUC, main GWP burdens arose from digestate storage, digestate application, and fugitive CH4 leakage during fermentation (Fig. 3; Table 4.1).

Anaerobic digestion increased national annual eutrophication and acidification burdens by 2.6 Gg PO4e and 11.4 Gg SO2e in 2014 excluding ILUC, with ranges of 1.8 to 3.4 Gg PO4e and 8.1 to 14.6 Gg SO2e after propagating major uncertainties (Table 5). Crops, food waste and "other waste" contributed approximately one third each towards eutro- phication burdens, whilst food waste accounted for 55% of acidification burdens owing to its volume and high NH3 emissions from NH4-rich digestate (Fig. 4). Notably, digestate storage was found to be a particular hotspot in our analyses, owing to the use of NH4-driven NH3 emission factors for different storage systems (Table 1; Misselbrook et al., 2012) which appear to be significantly higher than digestate storage emission factors reported by Amon et al. (2006) and applied in other studies (e.g. Lijó et al., 2014a, 2014b). The higher NH4-N content and pH of digestates compared with slurries would suggest that NH3 emissions from digestate storage are likely to be higher than for slurry storage.

Despite their relatively small quantities, brewery and bakery waste made significant net contributions to the eutrophication burden of the AD sector, again owing to diversion from animal feed, incurring additional wheat cultivation. Dominant contributing processes were, in order of decreasing importance, digestate storage, digestate application, cultivation (for EP only), and CHP combustion (Fig. 3; Table SI 4.2 and Table 4.3). Avoided manure management and avoided waste manage- ment represented the main acidification and eutrophication credits.

In 2014, AD reduced fossil resource depletion in the UK by 9.8 PJe, with a range of 8.9 to 10.8 PJ (Table 5). Food waste and crops contribut- ed 62% and 22%, respectively, towards fossil resource savings. Burdens arising from waste transport, crop cultivation and avoided manure application (avoided fertilizer replacement) were small compared with the credits arising from substitution of marginal grid electricity (Fig. 3; Table SI 4.4). Digestate application (fertilizer replacement) and the avoidance of waste composting generated small but significant FRDP credits (Fig. 3).

55: <header>3.3. Projected national balance of UK biogas sector </header>

In 2014, the AD sector reduced UK total GWP and FRDP burdens by 0.1% and 0.2%, and increased EP and AP burdens by 0.1% and 0.5%, respectively (Fig. 4). The net GWP reduction is close to zero at the top end of the uncertainty range, primarily owing to highly uncertain ILUC effects. The quantity of feedstock digested is projected to increase from 5134 Gg fresh matter in 2014 to 12,118 Gg fresh matter in 2017, with a similar composition (Table SI 2.1), leading to reductions of 0.3% and 0.5% in national GWP and FRDP burdens, and increases of 0.3% and 1.3% in national EP and AP burdens, respectively and excluding ILUC effects (Fig. 4). Food waste will continue to dominate GWP and FRDP credits and AP burden increases (Fig. 4), reflecting its relative vol- ume, avoided composting burdens and NH3 emissions from NH4-rich digestate. However, whilst the projected 2017 environmental balance was extrapolated assuming that new biogas plants reflect the current profile of the sector, 34 out of the 415 "in development" biogas plants as of 2014 were biomethane-to-grid plants (NNFCC, 2014b). Therefore, according to grid injection performance reported in Table 5, the GHG and FRDP balance of the biogas sector in 2017 may be slightly better than indicated in Fig. 4, though within error bar ranges.
56: <header>3.4. GHG abatement versus renewable energy generation </header>

Projected AD deployment will lead to a net reduction in national annual GHG emissions of 1595 Gg (1.59 Mt) CO2e, but will still only treat approximately 38% and 5%, respectively, of national food wastes and manures in 2017. Thus, there remains considerable technical poten- tial to achieve further GHG abatement through targeted expansion of AD. Treating all food waste generated and all handled (stored) manures in the UK through AD could lead to GHG abatement of 11,473 Gg (11.5 Mt) CO2e per year, a 5% reduction in national GHG emissions, and could avoid 93 PJ of fossil energy consumption. The diversion of other waste streams, such as industrial wastes, towards AD could increase this theoretical potential further. The economic feasibility of

digesting a large share of food waste and industrial wastes in large-scale AD plants is high, owing to gate fees, FiT income and economies of scale. However, whilst it may be economically feasible to install biogas plants on large pig, poultry and dairy farms, the 2015 FiT rate of £0.10 kWh-1 bioelectricity for biogas plants <250 kWe capacity is not sufficient to make small-scale manure AD economically viable (Mesa-Dominguez et al., 2015). Using the same CLCA methodology applied here, Mesa- Dominguez et al. (2015) estimated that digestion of all slurry from large UK dairy farms (>400 milking cows) could lead to GHG abatement of up to 0.574 Gg yr COe, equivalent to a saving of 3.3 kg CO2e for each kWh of bioelectricity generated. Even at a high FiT rate of £0.20 kWh- this would translate into an abatement cost of £60 per Mg CO2e. In comparison, large-scale maize-fed AD leads to net GHG abatement of just 0.16 kg CO2e kWh-1 excluding possible ILUC (Mesa-Dominguez et al., 2015), at a cost of £539 Mg CO2e at the 2015 FiT rate of £0.087 kWh-1 for biogas plants >500 kWe capacity (OFGEM, 2016). Results presented in Fig. 5 confirm that small-scale bio- gas plants fed with a high share of manure feedstock outperform the overall biogas sector in terms of GHG abatement, despite comparatively low energy yields. Furthermore, although large-scale maize AD-CHP performs comparatively well against other biogas feedstocks in terms of fossil energy substitution, Miscanthus heating pellets, solar PV panels and wind turbines substitute 2, 30 and 90 times more fossil energy per hectare of land utilized, at significantly lower cost (ADAS, 2014; Styles al., 2015b; Styles, 2015). Thus, there is a case to replace or supplement current FiT and RHI subsidies that incentivize large-scale crop-fed AD plants with incentives that prioritise efficient GHG abatement through deployment of manure- and waste-fed AD. In the UK, a recent RHI review has proposed that the proportion of biogas originating from crop feedstock be limited to 50% for individual plants to receive RHI payments on biomethane injected into the grid (DECC, 2016).

58: The environmental balance of AD deployment in the UK is highly sensitive to the marginal types of waste management and electricity generation replaced. Conservative assumptions regarding the type of waste management option substituted by AD were applied in this study (Table 4), and may underestimate the environmental benefits of AD deployment in the UK. 25% change in the aggregate environmental credit attributed to waste management substitution would change EP and GWP results by 13% and 11%, respectively (Table 5).

If policy intervention could ensure that AD bioelectricity substitutes electricity generated from coal, rather than natural gas, the environ- mental balance of national AD deployment would improve substantial- ly; GWP and FRDP credits would more than double (Table 5). Compared with current use of biogas in CHP plants, upgrading biomethane pro- duced in medium- large-scale digesters to substitute grid natural gas or transport diesel would increase GHG abatement by 20% and 38%, respectively. Similar improvements would be achieved for FRDP, with minor reductions in EP and AP burdens. Meanwhile, introducing a regulatory requirement to cover digestate stores could effectively mitigate net eutrophication and acidification burdens arising from AD (Table 5).
59: The UK biogas sector delivers important waste management, renew- able energy generation and nutrient recycling services, but requires the cultivation of crops and leads to significant emissions from digestate management. Accounting for these effects across multiple systems at a national level requires an expanded-boundary LCA approach, as concluded by Börjesson and Berglund (2007); Tonini et al. (2012) and Tufvesson et al. (2013). Consequential LCA supplements the detailed attributional LCA studies (Poeschl et al., 2012; Bacenetti et al., 2013; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Whiting and Azapagic, 2014; Lijó et al.,

2014b) that provide invaluable evidence to benchmark and optimize the operational efficiency of particular systems. Although involving considerable uncertainty, the application of consequential LCA with conservative (worst case) assumptions and uncertainty ranges provides an evidence base for the role of AD in national policies on climate change, renewable energy generation and waste management, consid- ering pertinent indirect effects and "unintended consequences".

The UK biogas sector delivers significant savings in GHG emissions and fossil resource depletion through replacement of: (i) grid- electricity; (ii) in-vessel composting of organic wastes; (iii) manure storage. The sector is projected to grow significantly, with FiT and RHI subsidies favouring more energy-efficient large-scale waste- and crop- fed biogas plants. However, there remains a large technical potential for further GHG abatement through expanded digestion of wastes and manures, whilst minimizing the digestion of crops that can lead to carbon leakage via indirect land use change. Our results also show that, where it is possible to use wastes to feed livestock (e.g. brewery and bakery wastes), this is a more environmentally efficient option than anaerobic digestion, supporting the conclusions of Tufvesson et al. (2013) and Tonini et al. (2016). Rather than basing FiT and RHI in- centives on plant size, these subsidies could be based on feedstock type, with higher subsides for manures, and waste feedstocks that cannot be more efficiently utilized as animal feed, in order to derive maximum en- vironmental benefit from AD. Landfill gate fees are partially responsible for driving expansion of AD; in the longer term, as landfilling of organic wastes is eliminated, differential fees (taxes) for waste management options could be based on their respective environmental efficiencies.

Eutrophication and acidification increases caused by the UK biogas sector are primarily attributable to somewhat uncertain NH3 emissions from digestate management, and fertilizer application to biogas-crops. Covering all digestate stores could largely mitigate net burdens caused by NH3 emissions, and would be a simple regulatory control point that could be checked during plant planning and commissioning.

Finally, two thirds of the net heat output from AD-CHP plants is dumped; upgrading biomethane to substitute grid natural gas or trans- port diesel would considerably improve the overall environmental balance of AD deployment in the UK. There is some evidence that new RHI subsidies are beginning to encourage the development of biomethane-to-grid plants in the UK (NNFCC, 2014b), but incentives for transport biomethane are so far lacking. Given recent evidence of high NOx emissions from modern diesel engines, there remains a need to evaluate possible air quality and health effects of diesel substitution with biomethane, which could represent an area for cost- effective policy support (simultaneously tackling climate change, energy security and air pollution threats). Biomethane is particularly well suited to powering heavy goods vehicles and buses that are less easily adapted to electric propulsion.

62: Whilst detailed attributional LCA provides a precise environmental profile of direct AD system effects useful for management decisions, consequential LCA provides a less precise but more complete environ- mental profile of the AD sector that captures indirect environmental ef- fects pertinent to policy making and regulation. Through the application of consequential LCA and the use of detailed feedstock input data and plant operating characteristics for the AD sector in the UK, this study highlighted the prevailing effects of counterfactual waste management and indirect land use change over biogas energy conversion efficiency in terms of GHG abatement. Accordingly, climate change policy priorities vis-a-vis AD should be to encourage digestion of food waste and ma- nures, whilst restricting digestion of crop inputs and wastes that could be used as animal feed - even if this involves the deployment of smaller biogas plants with lower energy conversion efficiency for manures. It may be wise to focus on AD policy in terms of waste management, rath- er than renewable energy generation - there are far more efficient

renewable energy options in terms of cost and land requirements. Nonetheless, upgrading the biogas produced for use as a transport fuel would considerably improve the environmental profile of the AD sector, compared with current dominant use for electricity generation and also compared with injection of upgraded biomethane into the gas grid. Biogas plant design, specifically the prevalence of open tanks and lagoons for digestate storage, is a dominant factor behind significant net eutrophication and acidification loadings from the UK biogas sector. Regulations requiring covered digestate stores, and injection applica- tion of digestate, could largely mitigate this problem.|DEFRA, 2014b. 64: Whilst detailed attributional LCA provides a precise environmental profile of direct AD system effects useful for management decisions, consequential LCA provides a less precise but more complete environ- mental profile of the AD sector that captures indirect environmental ef- fects pertinent to policy making and regulation. Through the application of consequential LCA and the use of detailed feedstock input data and plant operating characteristics for the AD sector in the UK, this study highlighted the prevailing effects of counterfactual waste management and indirect land use change over biogas energy conversion efficiency in terms of GHG abatement. Accordingly, climate change policy priorities vis-a-vis AD should be to encourage digestion of food waste and ma- nures, whilst restricting digestion of crop inputs and wastes that could be used as animal feed - even if this involves the deployment of smaller biogas plants with lower energy conversion efficiency for manures. It may be wise to focus on AD policy in terms of waste management, rath- er than renewable energy generation - there are far more efficient

renewable energy options in terms of cost and land requirements. Nonetheless, upgrading the biogas produced for use as a transport fuel would considerably improve the environmental profile of the AD sector, compared with current dominant use for electricity generation and also compared with injection of upgraded biomethane into the gas grid. Biogas plant design, specifically the prevalence of open tanks and lagoons for digestate storage, is a dominant factor behind significant net eutrophication and acidification loadings from the UK biogas sector. Regulations requiring covered digestate stores, and injection applica- tion of digestate, could largely mitigate this problem.|DEFRA, 2014b. 66: Whilst detailed attributional LCA provides a precise environmental profile of direct AD system effects useful for management decisions, consequential LCA provides a less precise but more complete environ- mental profile of the AD sector that captures indirect environmental ef- fects pertinent to policy making and regulation. Through the application of consequential LCA and the use of detailed feedstock input data and plant operating characteristics for the AD sector in the UK, this study highlighted the prevailing effects of counterfactual waste management and indirect land use change over biogas energy conversion efficiency in terms of GHG abatement. Accordingly, climate change policy priorities vis-a-vis AD should be to encourage digestion of food waste and ma- nures, whilst restricting digestion of crop inputs and wastes that could be used as animal feed - even if this involves the deployment of smaller biogas plants with lower energy conversion efficiency for manures. It may be wise to focus on AD policy in terms of waste management, rath- er than renewable energy generation - there are far more efficient

renewable energy options in terms of cost and land requirements. Nonetheless, upgrading the biogas produced for use as a transport fuel would considerably improve the environmental profile of the AD sector, compared with current dominant use for electricity generation and also compared with injection of upgraded biomethane into the gas grid. Biogas plant design, specifically the prevalence of open tanks and lagoons for digestate storage, is a dominant factor behind significant net eutrophication and acidification loadings from the UK biogas sector. Regulations requiring covered digestate stores, and injection applica- tion of digestate, could largely mitigate this problem.|DEFRA, 2014b. 68: Whilst detailed attributional LCA provides a precise environmental profile of direct AD system effects useful for management decisions, consequential LCA provides a less precise but more complete environ- mental profile of the AD sector that captures indirect environmental ef- fects pertinent to policy making and regulation. Through the application of consequential LCA and the use of detailed feedstock input data and plant operating characteristics for the AD sector in the UK, this study highlighted the prevailing effects of counterfactual waste management and indirect land use change over biogas energy conversion efficiency in terms of GHG abatement. Accordingly, climate change policy priorities vis-a-vis AD should be to encourage digestion of food waste and ma- nures, whilst restricting digestion of crop inputs and wastes that could be used as animal feed - even if this involves the deployment of smaller biogas plants with lower energy conversion efficiency for manures. It may be wise to focus on AD policy in terms of waste management, rath- er than renewable energy generation - there are far more efficient

renewable energy options in terms of cost and land requirements. Nonetheless, upgrading the biogas produced for use as a transport fuel would considerably improve the environmental profile of the AD sector, compared with current dominant use for electricity generation and also compared with injection of upgraded biomethane into the gas grid. Biogas plant design, specifically the prevalence of open tanks and lagoons for digestate storage, is a dominant factor behind significant net eutrophication and acidification loadings from the UK biogas sector. Regulations requiring covered digestate stores, and injection applica- tion of digestate, could largely mitigate this problem.|DEFRA, 2014b. 70: Whilst detailed attributional LCA provides a precise environmental profile of direct AD system effects useful for management decisions, consequential LCA provides a less precise but more complete environ- mental profile of the AD sector that captures indirect environmental ef- fects pertinent to policy making and regulation. Through the application of consequential LCA and the use of detailed feedstock input data and plant operating characteristics for the AD sector in the UK, this study highlighted the prevailing effects of counterfactual waste management and indirect land use change over biogas energy conversion efficiency in terms of GHG abatement. Accordingly, climate change policy priorities vis-a-vis AD should be to encourage digestion of food waste and ma- nures, whilst restricting digestion of crop inputs and wastes that could be used as animal feed - even if this involves the deployment of smaller biogas plants with lower energy conversion efficiency for manures. It may be wise to focus on AD policy in terms of waste management, rath- er than renewable energy generation - there are far more efficient

renewable energy options in terms of cost and land requirements. Nonetheless, upgrading the biogas produced for use as a transport fuel would considerably improve the environmental profile of the AD sector, compared with current dominant use for electricity generation and also compared with injection of upgraded biomethane into the gas grid. Biogas plant design, specifically the prevalence of open tanks and lagoons for digestate storage, is a dominant factor behind significant net eutrophication and acidification loadings from the UK biogas sector. Regulations requiring covered digestate stores, and injection applica- tion of digestate, could largely mitigate this problem.|DEFRA, 2014b. 72: Whilst detailed attributional LCA provides a precise environmental profile of direct AD system effects useful for management decisions, consequential LCA provides a less precise but more complete environ- mental profile of the AD sector that captures indirect environmental ef- fects pertinent to policy making and regulation. Through the application of consequential LCA and the use of detailed feedstock input data and plant operating characteristics for the AD sector in the UK, this study highlighted the prevailing effects of counterfactual waste management and indirect land use change over biogas energy conversion efficiency in terms of GHG abatement. Accordingly, climate change policy priorities vis-a-vis AD should be to encourage digestion of food waste and ma- nures, whilst restricting digestion of crop inputs and wastes that could be used as animal feed - even if this involves the deployment of smaller biogas plants with lower energy conversion efficiency for manures. It may be wise to focus on AD policy in terms of waste management, rath- er than renewable energy generation - there are far more efficient

renewable energy options in terms of cost and land requirements. Nonetheless, upgrading the biogas produced for use as a transport fuel would considerably improve the environmental profile of the AD sector, compared with current dominant use for electricity generation and also compared with injection of upgraded biomethane into the gas grid. Biogas plant design, specifically the prevalence of open tanks and lagoons for digestate storage, is a dominant factor behind significant net eutrophication and acidification loadings from the UK biogas sector. Regulations requiring covered digestate stores, and injection applica- tion of digestate, could largely mitigate this problem.|DEFRA, 2014b. 74: Whilst detailed attributional LCA provides a precise environmental profile of direct AD system effects useful for management decisions, consequential LCA provides a less precise but more complete environ- mental profile of the AD sector that captures indirect environmental ef- fects pertinent to policy making and regulation. Through the application of consequential LCA and the use of detailed feedstock input data and plant operating characteristics for the AD sector in the UK, this study highlighted the prevailing effects of counterfactual waste management and indirect land use change over biogas energy conversion efficiency in terms of GHG abatement. Accordingly, climate change policy priorities vis-a-vis AD should be to encourage digestion of food waste and ma- nures, whilst restricting digestion of crop inputs and wastes that could be used as animal feed - even if this involves the deployment of smaller biogas plants with lower energy conversion efficiency for manures. It may be wise to focus on AD policy in terms of waste management, rath- er than renewable energy generation - there are far more efficient

renewable energy options in terms of cost and land requirements. Nonetheless, upgrading the biogas produced for use as a transport fuel would considerably improve the environmental profile of the AD sector, compared with current dominant use for electricity generation and also compared with injection of upgraded biomethane into the gas grid. Biogas plant design, specifically the prevalence of open tanks and lagoons for digestate storage, is a dominant factor behind significant net eutrophication and acidification loadings from the UK biogas sector. Regulations requiring covered digestate stores, and injection applica- tion of digestate, could largely mitigate this problem.|DEFRA, 2014b. 76: Whilst detailed attributional LCA provides a precise environmental profile of direct AD system effects useful for management decisions, consequential LCA provides a less precise but more complete environ- mental profile of the AD sector that captures indirect environmental ef- fects pertinent to policy making and regulation. Through the application of consequential LCA and the use of detailed feedstock input data and plant operating characteristics for the AD sector in the UK, this study highlighted the prevailing effects of counterfactual waste management and indirect land use change over biogas energy conversion efficiency in terms of GHG abatement. Accordingly, climate change policy priorities vis-a-vis AD should be to encourage digestion of food waste and ma- nures, whilst restricting digestion of crop inputs and wastes that could be used as animal feed - even if this involves the deployment of smaller biogas plants with lower energy conversion efficiency for manures. It may be wise to focus on AD policy in terms of waste management, rath- er than renewable energy generation - there are far more efficient

renewable energy options in terms of cost and land requirements. Nonetheless, upgrading the biogas produced for use as a transport fuel would considerably improve the environmental profile of the AD sector, compared with current dominant use for electricity generation and also compared with injection of upgraded biomethane into the gas grid. Biogas plant design, specifically the prevalence of open tanks and lagoons for digestate storage, is a dominant factor behind significant net eutrophication and acidification loadings from the UK biogas sector. Regulations requiring covered digestate stores, and injection applica- tion of digestate, could largely mitigate this problem.|DEFRA, 2014b. 78: Whilst detailed attributional LCA provides a precise environmental profile of direct AD system effects useful for management decisions, consequential LCA provides a less precise but more complete environ- mental profile of the AD sector that captures indirect environmental ef- fects pertinent to policy making and regulation. Through the application of consequential LCA and the use of detailed feedstock input data and plant operating characteristics for the AD sector in the UK, this study highlighted the prevailing effects of counterfactual waste management and indirect land use change over biogas energy conversion efficiency in terms of GHG abatement. Accordingly, climate change policy priorities vis-a-vis AD should be to encourage digestion of food waste and ma- nures, whilst restricting digestion of crop inputs and wastes that could be used as animal feed - even if this involves the deployment of smaller biogas plants with lower energy conversion efficiency for manures. It may be wise to focus on AD policy in terms of waste management, rath- er than renewable energy generation - there are far more efficient

renewable energy options in terms of cost and land requirements. Nonetheless, upgrading the biogas produced for use as a transport fuel would considerably improve the environmental profile of the AD sector, compared with current dominant use for electricity generation and also compared with injection of upgraded biomethane into the gas grid. Biogas plant design, specifically the prevalence of open tanks and lagoons for digestate storage, is a dominant factor behind significant net eutrophication and acidification loadings from the UK biogas sector. Regulations requiring covered digestate stores, and injection applica- tion of digestate, could largely mitigate this problem.|DEFRA, 2014b. 80: Whilst detailed attributional LCA provides a precise environmental profile of direct AD system effects useful for management decisions, consequential LCA provides a less precise but more complete environ- mental profile of the AD sector that captures indirect environmental ef- fects pertinent to policy making and regulation. Through the application of consequential LCA and the use of detailed feedstock input data and plant operating characteristics for the AD sector in the UK, this study highlighted the prevailing effects of counterfactual waste management and indirect land use change over biogas energy conversion efficiency in terms of GHG abatement. Accordingly, climate change policy priorities vis-a-vis AD should be to encourage digestion of food waste and ma- nures, whilst restricting digestion of crop inputs and wastes that could be used as animal feed - even if this involves the deployment of smaller biogas plants with lower energy conversion efficiency for manures. It may be wise to focus on AD policy in terms of waste management, rath- er than renewable energy generation - there are far more efficient

renewable energy options in terms of cost and land requirements. Nonetheless, upgrading the biogas produced for use as a transport fuel would considerably improve the environmental profile of the AD sector, compared with current dominant use for electricity generation and also compared with injection of upgraded biomethane into the gas grid. Biogas plant design, specifically the prevalence of open tanks and lagoons for digestate storage, is a dominant factor behind significant net eutrophication and acidification loadings from the UK biogas sector. Regulations requiring covered digestate stores, and injection applica- tion of digestate, could largely mitigate this problem.|DEFRA, 2014b.
